skill.name: workflow-automation
skill.description: Automate repetitive development tasks and workflows. Use when creating build scripts, automating deployments, or setting up development workflows. Handles npm scripts, Makefile, GitHub Actions workflows, and task automation.
skill.summary: Automate repetitive development tasks and workflows. Use when creating build scripts, automating dep...
skill.author: supercent-io

security_audit.summary: This is a documentation and template skill providing workflow automation examples. All 43 static findings are false positives: the 'external_commands' detections are example code snippets in documentation, 'network' findings are external documentation links, 'filesystem' findings check for prerequisite tools, and 'sensitive' findings involve standard .env file creation from templates. No actual security risks present.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Execution in Documentation
dangerous_patterns.description: The static scanner detected 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' and 'shell command substitution' patterns in the skill. These are FALSE POSITIVES - they appear in example code blocks showing Makefile commands, shell script examples, and npm script configurations. This is a documentation skill that provides templates, not executable code that runs arbitrary commands.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network URLs in Documentation Links
dangerous_patterns.description: The static scanner detected 'Hardcoded URL' patterns at lines 302-304. These are FALSE POSITIVES - they are legitimate documentation links to external resources (npm scripts docs, Make tutorial, Husky documentation). No network requests are made by this skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: Filesystem Command Checks
dangerous_patterns.description: The static scanner detected 'Standard device file access' at lines 144 and 149. These are FALSE POSITIVES - they are 'command -v' checks that verify Node.js and Docker are installed before running setup scripts. This is standard practice for prerequisite checking in development automation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Environment File Creation
dangerous_patterns.description: The static scanner detected 'Environment file access' at lines 159-162. These are FALSE POSITIVES - they show the standard practice of copying .env.example to .env for development setup. This is a safe, intentional pattern for initializing environment configuration.

dangerous_patterns.title: Critical Heuristic - False Alarm
dangerous_patterns.description: The scanner flagged 'DANGEROUS COMBINATION: Code execution + Network + Credential access'. This is a FALSE POSITIVE - the skill contains separate examples of shell commands (for automation), documentation links (for reference), and mentions .env files (as templates). These are not combined in a malicious pattern. This is a documentation skill providing workflow templates.

content.value_statement: Developers spend hours on repetitive tasks like running builds, tests, and deployments. This skill provides ready-to-use templates for npm scripts, Makefiles, Git hooks, and CI/CD pipelines to automate these workflows.
actual_capabilities: Generate npm script configurations for common development tasks
Create Makefiles with platform-agnostic build commands
Set up Git hooks with Husky for pre-commit quality checks
Configure GitHub Actions workflows for automated CI/CD
Write shell scripts for development environment setup
limitations: Does not execute commands - provides templates only
Does not integrate with specific CI providers beyond GitHub Actions
Requires manual adaptation for non-Node.js projects

use_cases.title: Automate Build and Test Commands
use_cases.description: Configure npm scripts and Makefiles to run builds, tests, and linting with single commands
use_cases.target_user: Individual developers wanting to speed up their workflow

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Development Environment
use_cases.description: Set up consistent git hooks and scripts so all team members run the same quality checks
use_cases.target_user: Development teams needing consistent coding standards

use_cases.title: Create CI/CD Pipelines
use_cases.description: Generate GitHub Actions workflows that automatically run tests and deployments on code changes
use_cases.target_user: DevOps engineers setting up continuous integration

prompt_templates.title: Basic npm Scripts Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Create npm scripts for a TypeScript project with scripts for dev, build, test, and lint commands
prompt_templates.scenario: Starting a new Node.js project and want standard scripts

prompt_templates.title: Makefile for Cross-Platform Builds
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a Makefile with targets for help, install, build, test, and clean that works on Linux and macOS

prompt_templates.title: Git Pre-commit Hook Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Set up Husky with lint-staged to run ESLint and Prettier on staged TypeScript files before commit
prompt_templates.scenario: Automating code quality checks on git commits

prompt_templates.title: Full CI/CD Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a GitHub Actions workflow that installs dependencies, runs linting, executes tests with coverage, and builds the application on push to main
prompt_templates.scenario: Setting up automated testing and deployment

output_examples.input: Create npm scripts for running tests with coverage
output_examples.output: A package.json scripts section with test command that runs Jest with coverage reporting

output_examples.input: Generate a Makefile for a Node.js project
output_examples.output: A Makefile with .PHONY targets for install, dev, build, test, and clean with proper dependencies

best_practices: Keep scripts idempotent so they can be run multiple times safely
Add clear error messages and exit codes for failure scenarios
Document each script with comments explaining what it does
anti_patterns: Hardcoding secrets or API keys directly in scripts
Running destructive commands like rm -rf without user confirmation
Creating scripts that depend on specific local environment configurations

faq.question: What platforms does this skill support?
faq.answer: This skill works with Claude, Codex, Claude Code and other AI coding assistants. The generated scripts work on Linux, macOS, and Windows with WSL.

faq.question: Can I use this for non-Node.js projects?
faq.answer: The npm script examples require Node.js, but the Makefile, shell scripts, and GitHub Actions templates can be adapted for any programming language.

faq.question: Do the scripts include error handling?
faq.answer: The templates include basic error handling with set -e for bash scripts and clear exit codes, but you should review and enhance for production use.

faq.question: How do I add custom scripts?
faq.answer: Follow the existing patterns in the templates. Add new entries to package.json scripts, new targets to the Makefile, or new steps to the GitHub Actions workflow.

faq.question: Can I use this with other CI providers?
faq.answer: The skill focuses on GitHub Actions, but the concepts transfer to GitLab CI, CircleCI, or Jenkins with appropriate syntax changes.

faq.question: Are these scripts safe to run in production?
faq.answer: The templates are starting points. Always review scripts before running in production, especially deployment scripts that modify remote servers.
